# COMP 110 Data Analysis for Continuous Improvement

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->

<!--<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/> -->

## Idea

This course should spend more time on each topic or cover fewer topics because it will slow the pace for students who don't have prior coding experience.

### Support 1

Majority of students responded that they have less than a year of programming experience with 328 students reporting having none to less than one month of experience before taking COMP 110. With many students new to programming and COMP 110 being their first introduction to it, the course's pace should be slow to ensure understanding of key fundamental concepts.

<img src="/static/imgs/bar_plot.png" alt="Bar plot of levels of programming experience."  width="1000"/>

### Support 2

Students who report the pace of the class as a 4 out of 7 most frequently reported an understanding of a 5 out of 7. This shows that students that believe the course is moving at an appropriate pace report higher levels of understanding.

<img src="/static/imgs/histogram.png" alt="Histogram."  width="1000"/>

### Support 3

There is a positive correlation between reported understanding and students that would recommend COMP 110 to future students. This shows that if we can improve students' understanding by slowing the pace, they would feel more satisfied with the course and be more likely to recommend it to others.

<img src="/static/imgs/line_plot_2.png" alt="Line graph."  width="1000"/>

## Conlcusions

I recommend that the pace of COMP 110 is slowed for following classes. Data analysis shows that most students are coming into the course with little to no progamming experience. A histogram visualization shows that a large frequence of students that report a pace of a 4 or a 5 also report a middle level of understanding around a 4 or a 5. This supports the conclusion that a medium pace is more condusive to understanding than a level 7 pace is to a level 7 understanding for example. Additionally, there is a positive correlation between understanding and would_recommend showing that if students are more likely to understand the content, they are more likely to recommend the course to others. This supports the idea as it would have a positive impact on the success of the course as more students would take it. The biggest cost of this proposed change is that certain topics would likely have to be ommitted from the course to allow more time to be spent on each topic. This may negatively impact other courses in the major/minor computer science pathway as they might have to include the omitted lessons. Further analysis can be done to determine if there is a correlation between level of programming experience and the pace they believe the course is at. A scatter plot with a regression line could also be explored to find the exact R squared value of the positive correlation identified.
